comment_text.sn12.66:1.7: Sammasa only appears in one other case in early Pali, at Thag 1.23:1.3. The root is mas (“to rub”), suggesting the idea of examining with the fingers or “to probe”.
comment_text.sn12.66:3.6: “Attachment” is upadhi, which is both the things of the world to which we cling, and the inner clinging (see Snp 1.2, MN 105:28.2.). The Jains defined upadhi similarly (Tattvārthasūtra 9.26). The dominant meaning in Sanskrit is “fraud”, which perhaps informs the Buddhist sense: the things we attach to give a false sense of security. Here it plays the role of “grasping” (upādāna).
comment_text.sn12.66:13.10: The drinks are surā (beer), dadhimaṇḍaka (buttermilk or whey), maṭṭhaloṇikā (salted whey or sour cream, i.e. lassi), and loṇasovīraka (salted infusion of fermented barley and herbs). These correspond with drinks mentioned, along with others, in the Cāraka Saṁhita as surā, mastu (Hindi maṭṭhā), dadhimaṇḍa, and sauvīraka. These were fermented drinks used as purgatives, and some or all were also recreational liquors. | Ayaṁ surāpipāsitā pānīyena vā vinetuṁ seems corrupt, read: ayaṁ pipāsā surāpānīyena vā vinetuṁ (“to dispel this thirst by a drink of beer”). | Loṇasovīraka is allowed as medicine (Bu Pj 3:5.33.14, Kd 6:16.3.1; preparation methods are given in Suśruta Saṁhitā 1.44 and commentary to Pj 3). It probably originated in Sovīra (DN 19:36.6). Sanskrit just has sauvīraka but Cāraka Saṁhita 3.1.18 says it can be drunk with salt; salted drinks are still popular in India. It was a great favorite of the hard-partying women of Madra, who, it was said, would sooner part with husband or son than a drop of sauvīraka (Mahābhārata 8.30). | The last phrase is corrupt, read ahitāya dukkhāya following PTS and Bodhi.

root_text.sn12.66:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kurūsu viharati kammāsadhammaṁ nāma kurūnaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn12.66:1.8: Evaṁ vutte, aññataro bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.66:1.10: “Yathā kathaṁ pana tvaṁ, bhikkhu, sammasasi antaraṁ sammasan”ti?
root_text.sn12.66:1.12: Yathā so bhikkhu byākāsi na so bhikkhu bhagavato cittaṁ ārādhesi.
root_text.sn12.66:2.1: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā ānando bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.66:2.3: yaṁ bhagavā antaraṁ sammasaṁ bhāseyya. Bhagavato sutvā bhikkhū dhāressantī”ti.
root_text.sn12.66:2.5: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:3.1: “Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sammasamāno sammasati antaraṁ sammasaṁ:
root_text.sn12.66:3.2: ‘yaṁ kho idaṁ anekavidhaṁ nānappakārakaṁ dukkhaṁ loke uppajjati jarāmaraṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:3.3: Idaṁ kho dukkhaṁ kiṁnidānaṁ kiṁsamudayaṁ kiṁjātikaṁ kiṁpabhavaṁ, kismiṁ sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, kismiṁ asati jarāmaraṇaṁ na hotī’ti?
root_text.sn12.66:3.5: ‘yaṁ kho idaṁ anekavidhaṁ nānappakārakaṁ dukkhaṁ loke uppajjati jarāmaraṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:3.6: Idaṁ kho dukkhaṁ upadhinidānaṁ upadhisamudayaṁ upadhijātikaṁ upadhipabhavaṁ, upadhismiṁ sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, upadhismiṁ asati jarāmaraṇaṁ na hotī’ti.
root_text.sn12.66:3.7: So jarāmaraṇañca pajānāti jarāmaraṇasamudayañca pajānāti jarāmaraṇanirodhañca pajānāti yā ca jarāmaraṇanirodhasāruppagāminī paṭipadā tañca pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.66:3.9: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno jarāmaraṇanirodhāya.
root_text.sn12.66:4.2: ‘upadhi panāyaṁ kiṁnidāno kiṁsamudayo kiṁjātiko kiṁpabhavo, kismiṁ sati upadhi hoti, kismiṁ asati upadhi na hotī’ti?
root_text.sn12.66:4.4: ‘upadhi taṇhānidāno taṇhāsamudayo taṇhājātiko taṇhāpabhavo, taṇhāya sati upadhi hoti, taṇhāya asati upadhi na hotī’ti.
root_text.sn12.66:4.5: So upadhiñca pajānāti upadhisamudayañca pajānāti upadhinirodhañca pajānāti yā ca upadhinirodhasāruppagāminī paṭipadā tañca pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.66:4.7: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno upadhinirodhāya.
root_text.sn12.66:5.2: ‘taṇhā panāyaṁ kattha uppajjamānā uppajjati, kattha nivisamānā nivisatī’ti?
root_text.sn12.66:5.4: yaṁ kho loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ etthesā taṇhā uppajjamānā uppajjati, ettha nivisamānā nivisati.
root_text.sn12.66:5.6: Cakkhuṁ loke piyarūpaṁ, sātarūpaṁ, etthesā taṇhā uppajjamānā uppajjati, ettha nivisamānā nivisati.
root_text.sn12.66:5.11: mano loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ, etthesā taṇhā uppajjamānā uppajjati ettha nivisamānā nivisati.
root_text.sn12.66:6.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ niccato addakkhuṁ sukhato addakkhuṁ attato addakkhuṁ ārogyato addakkhuṁ khemato addakkhuṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:6.3: Ye taṇhaṁ vaḍḍhesuṁ te upadhiṁ vaḍḍhesuṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:6.4: Ye upadhiṁ vaḍḍhesuṁ te dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhesuṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:6.5: Ye dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhesuṁ te na parimucciṁsu jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘na parimucciṁsu dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:7.1: Yepi hi keci, bhikkhave, anāgatamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ niccato dakkhissanti sukhato dakkhissanti attato dakkhissanti ārogyato dakkhissanti khemato dakkhissanti.
root_text.sn12.66:7.3: Ye taṇhaṁ vaḍḍhissanti te upadhiṁ vaḍḍhissanti.
root_text.sn12.66:7.4: Ye upadhiṁ vaḍḍhissanti te dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhissanti.
root_text.sn12.66:7.5: Ye dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhissanti te na parimuccissanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘na parimuccissanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:8.1: Yepi hi keci, bhikkhave, etarahi samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ niccato passanti sukhato passanti attato passanti ārogyato passanti khemato passanti.
root_text.sn12.66:8.3: Ye taṇhaṁ vaḍḍhenti te upadhiṁ vaḍḍhenti.
root_text.sn12.66:8.4: Ye upadhiṁ vaḍḍhenti te dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhenti.
root_text.sn12.66:8.5: Ye dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhenti te na parimuccanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘na parimuccanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:9.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, āpānīyakaṁso vaṇṇasampanno gandhasampanno rasasampanno.
root_text.sn12.66:9.3: Atha puriso āgaccheyya ghammābhitatto ghammapareto kilanto tasito pipāsito.
root_text.sn12.66:9.5: ‘ayaṁ te, ambho purisa, āpānīyakaṁso vaṇṇasampanno gandhasampanno rasasampanno;
root_text.sn12.66:9.8: Pivato hi kho taṁ chādessati vaṇṇenapi gandhenapi rasenapi, pivitvā ca pana tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchasi maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhan’ti.
root_text.sn12.66:9.9: So taṁ āpānīyakaṁsaṁ sahasā appaṭisaṅkhā piveyya, nappaṭinissajjeyya.
root_text.sn12.66:9.10: So tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigaccheyya maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:9.11: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, ye hi keci atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ …pe…
root_text.sn12.66:9.13: etarahi samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ niccato passanti sukhato passanti attato passanti ārogyato passanti khemato passanti, te taṇhaṁ vaḍḍhenti.
root_text.sn12.66:9.14: Ye taṇhaṁ vaḍḍhenti te upadhiṁ vaḍḍhenti.
root_text.sn12.66:9.15: Ye upadhiṁ vaḍḍhenti te dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhenti.
root_text.sn12.66:9.16: Ye dukkhaṁ vaḍḍhenti te na parimuccanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘na parimuccanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:10.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ aniccato addakkhuṁ dukkhato addakkhuṁ anattato addakkhuṁ rogato addakkhuṁ bhayato addakkhuṁ,
root_text.sn12.66:10.3: Ye taṇhaṁ pajahiṁsu te upadhiṁ pajahiṁsu.
root_text.sn12.66:10.4: Ye upadhiṁ pajahiṁsu te dukkhaṁ pajahiṁsu.
root_text.sn12.66:10.5: Ye dukkhaṁ pajahiṁsu te parimucciṁsu jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘parimucciṁsu dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:11.1: Yepi hi keci, bhikkhave, anāgatamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ aniccato dakkhissanti dukkhato dakkhissanti anattato dakkhissanti rogato dakkhissanti bhayato dakkhissanti,
root_text.sn12.66:12.1: Yepi hi keci, bhikkhave, etarahi samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ aniccato passanti dukkhato passanti anattato passanti rogato passanti bhayato passanti,
root_text.sn12.66:12.3: Ye taṇhaṁ pajahanti te upadhiṁ pajahanti.
root_text.sn12.66:12.4: Ye upadhiṁ pajahanti te dukkhaṁ pajahanti.
root_text.sn12.66:12.5: Ye dukkhaṁ pajahanti te parimuccanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘parimuccanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:13.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, āpānīyakaṁso vaṇṇasampanno gandhasampanno rasasampanno.
root_text.sn12.66:13.3: Atha puriso āgaccheyya ghammābhitatto ghammapareto kilanto tasito pipāsito.
root_text.sn12.66:13.5: ‘ayaṁ te, ambho purisa, āpānīyakaṁso vaṇṇasampanno gandhasampanno rasasampanno
root_text.sn12.66:13.8: Pivato hi kho taṁ chādessati vaṇṇenapi gandhenapi rasenapi; pivitvā ca pana tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchasi maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhan’ti.
root_text.sn12.66:13.9: Atha kho, bhikkhave, tassa purisassa evamassa:
root_text.sn12.66:13.10: ‘sakkā kho me ayaṁ surāpipāsitā pānīyena vā vinetuṁ dadhimaṇḍakena vā vinetuṁ bhaṭṭhaloṇikāya vā vinetuṁ loṇasovīrakena vā vinetuṁ, na tvevāhaṁ taṁ piveyyaṁ, yaṁ mama assa dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāyā’ti.
root_text.sn12.66:13.11: So taṁ āpānīyakaṁsaṁ paṭisaṅkhā na piveyya, paṭinissajjeyya.
root_text.sn12.66:13.12: So tatonidānaṁ na maraṇaṁ vā nigaccheyya maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.66:13.13: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, ye hi keci atītamaddhānaṁ samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ aniccato addakkhuṁ dukkhato addakkhuṁ anattato addakkhuṁ rogato addakkhuṁ bhayato addakkhuṁ,
root_text.sn12.66:13.15: Ye taṇhaṁ pajahiṁsu te upadhiṁ pajahiṁsu.
root_text.sn12.66:13.16: Ye upadhiṁ pajahiṁsu te dukkhaṁ pajahiṁsu.
root_text.sn12.66:13.17: Ye dukkhaṁ pajahiṁsu te parimucciṁsu jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘parimucciṁsu dukkhasmā’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.66:14.2: etarahi samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā yaṁ loke piyarūpaṁ sātarūpaṁ taṁ aniccato passanti dukkhato passanti anattato passanti rogato passanti bhayato passanti,
root_text.sn12.66:14.4: Ye taṇhaṁ pajahanti te upadhiṁ pajahanti.
root_text.sn12.66:14.5: Ye upadhiṁ pajahanti te dukkhaṁ pajahanti.
root_text.sn12.66:14.6: Ye dukkhaṁ pajahanti te parimuccanti jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, ‘parimuccanti dukkhasmā’ti vadāmī”ti.

translation_text.sn12.66:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kurus, near the Kuru town named Kammāsadamma.
translation_text.sn12.66:1.3: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn12.66:1.8: When he said this, one of the mendicants said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn12.66:1.10: “But mendicant, how do you probe within?”
translation_text.sn12.66:1.12: but the Buddha was not happy with the answer.
translation_text.sn12.66:2.1: When he had spoken, Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn12.66:2.2: “Now is the time, Blessed One! Now is the time, Holy One!
translation_text.sn12.66:2.3: Let the Buddha speak of probing within. The mendicants will listen and remember it.”
translation_text.sn12.66:2.4: “Well then, Ānanda, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn12.66:3.2: ‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms.
translation_text.sn12.66:3.3: But what is the source of this suffering? When what exists do old age and death come to be? And when what does not exist do old age and death not come to be?’
translation_text.sn12.66:3.5: ‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms.
translation_text.sn12.66:3.6: The source of this suffering is attachment. When attachments exist old age and death come to be. And when attachments do not exist old age and death don’t come to be.’
translation_text.sn12.66:3.7: They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation.
translation_text.sn12.66:3.8: And they practice in line with that path.
translation_text.sn12.66:3.9: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.66:4.2: ‘But what is the source of this attachment? When what exists does attachment come to be? And when what does not exist does attachment not come to be?’
translation_text.sn12.66:4.4: ‘The source of this attachment is craving. When craving exists attachments come to be. And when craving doesn’t exist attachments don’t come to be.’
translation_text.sn12.66:4.5: They understand attachments, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation.
translation_text.sn12.66:4.6: And they practice in line with that path.
translation_text.sn12.66:4.7: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of attachments.
translation_text.sn12.66:5.2: ‘But where does that craving arise and where does it settle?’
translation_text.sn12.66:5.4: ‘That craving arises and settles on whatever in the world seems nice and pleasant.
translation_text.sn12.66:5.5: And what in the world seems nice and pleasant?
translation_text.sn12.66:5.6: The eye in the world seems nice and pleasant, and it is there that craving arises and settles.
translation_text.sn12.66:5.11: mind in the world seems nice and pleasant, and it is there that craving arises and settles.’
translation_text.sn12.66:6.1: There were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe.
translation_text.sn12.66:6.3: As their craving grew, their attachments grew.
translation_text.sn12.66:6.4: As their attachments grew, their suffering grew.
translation_text.sn12.66:6.5: And as their suffering grew, they were not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were not freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:7.1: There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future who will see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe.
translation_text.sn12.66:7.3: As their craving grows, their attachments will grow.
translation_text.sn12.66:7.4: As their attachments grow, their suffering will grow.
translation_text.sn12.66:7.5: And as their suffering grows, they will not be freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They will not be freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:8.1: There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe.
translation_text.sn12.66:8.3: As their craving grows, their attachments grow.
translation_text.sn12.66:8.4: As their attachments grow, their suffering grows.
translation_text.sn12.66:8.5: And as their suffering grows, they are not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are not freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.1: Suppose there was a bronze goblet of beverage that had a nice color, aroma, and flavor.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.2: But it was mixed with poison.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.3: Then along comes a man struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.5: ‘Here, my friend, this bronze goblet of beverage has a nice color, aroma, and flavor.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.8: If you drink it, the color, aroma, and flavor will be appetizing, but it will result in death or deadly pain.’
translation_text.sn12.66:9.9: He wouldn’t put that beverage down. Hastily, without reflection, he’d drink it,
translation_text.sn12.66:9.10: resulting in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.11: In the same way, there are ascetics and brahmins of the past …
translation_text.sn12.66:9.13: There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as permanent, as pleasurable, as self, as healthy, and as safe. Their craving grows.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.14: As their craving grows, their attachments grow.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.15: As their attachments grow, their suffering grows.
translation_text.sn12.66:9.16: And as their suffering grows, they are not freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are not freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:10.1: There were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous.
translation_text.sn12.66:10.3: Giving up craving, they gave up attachments.
translation_text.sn12.66:10.4: Giving up attachments, they gave up suffering.
translation_text.sn12.66:10.5: Giving up suffering, they were freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:11.1: There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future who will see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous.
translation_text.sn12.66:11.4: they will be freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:12.1: There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous.
translation_text.sn12.66:12.3: Giving up craving, they give up attachments.
translation_text.sn12.66:12.4: Giving up attachments, they give up suffering.
translation_text.sn12.66:12.5: Giving up suffering, they are freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.1: Suppose there was a bronze goblet of beverage that had a nice color, aroma, and flavor.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.2: But it was mixed with poison.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.3: Then along comes a man struggling in the oppressive heat, weary, thirsty, and parched.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.5: ‘Here, my friend, this bronze goblet of beverage has a nice color, aroma, and flavor.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.8: If you drink it, its nice color, aroma, and flavor will refresh you. But drinking it will result in death or deadly pain.’
translation_text.sn12.66:13.10: ‘I can allay my thirst with a fermented drink of beer, buttermilk, salt lassi, or salted infusion. But in any case, I shouldn’t drink that beverage, for it would be for my lasting harm and suffering.’
translation_text.sn12.66:13.11: He’d put that beverage down. After appraisal, he wouldn’t drink it,
translation_text.sn12.66:13.12: and it wouldn’t result in death or deadly pain.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.13: In the same way, there were ascetics and brahmins of the past who saw the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.15: Giving up craving, they gave up attachments.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.16: Giving up attachments, they gave up suffering.
translation_text.sn12.66:13.17: Giving up suffering, they were freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They were freed from suffering, I say.
translation_text.sn12.66:14.1: There will be ascetics and brahmins in the future …
translation_text.sn12.66:14.2: There are ascetics and brahmins in the present who see the things that seem nice and pleasant in the world as impermanent, as suffering, as not-self, as diseased, and as dangerous.
translation_text.sn12.66:14.4: Giving up craving, they give up attachments.
translation_text.sn12.66:14.5: Giving up attachments, they give up suffering.
translation_text.sn12.66:14.6: Giving up suffering, they are freed from rebirth, old age, and death, from sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. They are freed from suffering, I say.”

variant_text.sn12.66:13.10: surāpipāsitā → surāpipāsā (?) | bhaṭṭhaloṇikāya → matthaloṇikāya (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); maṭṭhaloṇikāya (sya-all, km)
